attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic eight days following excision of a basal cell carcinoma from her right forearm with increasing pain and redness around her surgical wound. She completed her prescribed five-day course of oral antibiotics following surgery. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as a teacher.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The surgical wound on the lateral aspect of her right forearm shows a 4 cm area of induration with surrounding erythema and tenderness. The area feels firm but it is difficult to determine if there is underlying fluctuance due to the anatomical location. Her wound edges are intact with no obvious discharge.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic test to request next?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs of post-operative wound infection following skin cancer excision. The clinical findings of pain, erythema and induration are concerning, but the physical examination is limited in determining whether there is an underlying collection.

Ultrasound imaging is the most appropriate next investigation as it can reliably differentiate between cellulitis and abscess, and can guide subsequent management decisions. It is non-invasive, readily available, and can accurately determine the presence, size and depth of any collection.

Wound swab would not be helpful at this stage as there is no discharge and the wound edges are intact. Swabs should be taken if purulent material is encountered during drainage of a confirmed collection.

Blood cultures would only be indicated if there were systemic symptoms or signs of sepsis, which are not present in this case. The patient appears well with normal vital signs.

Magnetic resonance imaging would be excessive for a superficial post-operative wound infection. While MRI provides excellent soft tissue detail, it is expensive and unnecessary when ultrasound can provide adequate information.

Computed tomography would not be helpful in this situation as it involves unnecessary radiation exposure and is less accurate than ultrasound for evaluating superficial soft tissue collections. CT would only be indicated if there was concern about deep tissue involvement or complications.

If ultrasound confirms an abscess, incision and drainage would be indicated. If only cellulitis is present, then appropriate antibiotic therapy can be commenced.
learningPoints: Ultrasound is the preferred initial imaging modality for suspected post-operative soft tissue infection to differentiate abscess from cellulitis
Clinical assessment alone may not reliably distinguish between cellulitis and abscess in deeper post-operative infections
references: Moffarah AS, Al Mohajer M, Hurwitz BL, Armstrong DG. Skin and Soft Tissue Infections. Microbiol Spectr. 2016;4(4):10.1128/microbiolspec.DMIH2-0014-2015. doi:10.1128/microbiolspec.DMIH2-0014-2015
Sukumaran V, Senanayake S. Bacterial skin and soft tissue infections. Aust Prescr. 2016;39(5):159-163. doi:10.18773/austprescr.2016.058
